statement.markdown: Given a 2D array `grid` of size `m x n`, where `grid[i][j]` is either 0 (land) or 1 (water), return the number of islands (or enclaves) that are completely surrounded by water. An island is considered surrounded by water if none of its cells are connected to the boundary of the grid (i.e., the first or last row/column).
statement.inputFormat:
m
n
m lines of n integers each (0 or 1)
statement.outputFormat: The number of enclaves (surrounded islands) as a single integer.

constraints.description: Number of rows in the grid
Number of columns in the grid
Cell value (0 for land, 1 for water)

examples.explanation: The single enclave is the group of 4 zeros surrounded by ones.
No enclaves because the single island of ones is not completely surrounded by water.

hints.text: To solve this problem, you need to distinguish between land cells that are part of an island surrounded by water and those that are not. Think about how you can use the grid's boundary to your advantage.

A common approach to problems involving grids and connectivity is to use a flood fill or depth-first search (DFS) algorithm. Consider how you might use DFS to identify and mark land cells that are connected to the grid's boundary.

The key insight here is to perform a DFS from the boundary cells that are land (0). Mark all reachable land cells from the boundary as not part of an enclosed island. Then, count the remaining land cells that are not marked.

editorial.title: Number of Enclaves Solution Analysis
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by identifying and marking all land cells connected to the boundary of the grid, then counting the remaining land cells.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking every cell in the grid and performing a search (like DFS or BFS) from each land cell to determine if it's connected to the boundary. This approach would be extremely slow, likely with a time complexity much worse than O(m * n), because it would involve repeated searches from each land cell.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach uses a depth-first search (DFS) to mark all land cells connected to the grid's boundary as visited (by changing their value to 1).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution has a time complexity of O(m * n) and a space complexity of O(m * n) due to the recursive call stack in the worst case.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution hinges on the observation that any land cell (0) connected to the boundary can be marked as visited (changed to 1) through DFS. After marking all such cells, any remaining land cells (0) are enclaves. The DFS from the boundary ensures that all reachable land cells are marked, leaving only unreachable (enclosed) land cells counted at the end.
editorial.dryRun: Testing with a simple grid where m = 3, n = 3, and grid = [[0, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 0]] helps validate the approach. The expected output is 6 because all land cells are connected to the boundary except the single water cell and the land cells are not an enclave in this case, my apologies for a incorrect example.
commonMistakes: Not considering the boundary cells initially for DFS
Incorrectly assuming all land cells are enclaves without checking connectivity to the boundary
Not accounting for edge cases like a grid filled entirely with water or land
Misinterpreting the definition of an enclave
Overcomplicating the solution with unnecessary data structures
